Title: Dihydroartemisinic acid dehydrogenase-mediated alternative route for artemisinin biosynthesis

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species: Artemisia annua; Saccharomyces cerevisiae; Nicotiana benthamiana
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Artemisia annua; Saccharomyces cerevisiae; Nicotiana benthamiana
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Downstream/terminal branch from artemisinic acid to dihydroartemisinic acid
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes bidirectional conversion between artemisinic acid and dihydroartemisinic acid, preferentially reducing the Δ11(13) double bond of artemisinic acid to form dihydroartemisinic acid.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms key intermediate DHAA required for artemisinin formation
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control step in alternative DHAA-forming route; supported as key oxidoreductase but not explicitly claimed rate-limiting
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes DHAA accumulation and consequently increases artemisinin accumulation while decreasing AA when overexpressed; RNAi causes the opposite trend.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Antimalarial drug; also reported anti-tumor, immunomodulatory, anti-inflammatory and antiviral activities
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Glandular hairs of above-ground parts of Artemisia annua, highest in leaves
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Activity-guided protein purification; proteomics; heterologous expression in E. coli; transient expression in Nicotiana benthamiana; enzyme assays; kinetic analysis; site-directed mutagenesis; subcellular localization; overexpression and RNAi in Artemisia annua cells; metabolite profiling; heterologous pathway reconstruction in S. cerevisiae
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: UV treatment in N. benthamiana was used downstream to accelerate DHAA auto-oxidation to artemisinin; enzyme assays optimized at pH 7.5 and 30 °C
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: CaMV35S promoter in A. annua cells and N. benthamiana; THI2 promoter for extra copy expression in S. cerevisiae
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Proteomics (LC-MS/MS of DEAE-2 active fraction), PRIDE: PXD062563
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Some transformed A. annua cells initially grew slowly or died under selection; no clear intrinsic metabolic penalty of AaDHAADH itself established
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Identified by activity-guided purification rather than co-expression analysis. DBR2, despite high similarity, showed no activity toward AA or DHAA. AaDHAADH shares 94.32% amino acid similarity with DBR2 but differs in C-terminus, function and localization.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AaDHAADH from Artemisia annua was identified as the previously unknown enzyme catalyzing bidirectional conversion between AA and DHAA. 2. It preferentially catalyzes AA reduction to DHAA, supporting an alternative route toward artemisinin biosynthesis. 3. Overexpression in A. annua cells increased DHAA and artemisinin and decreased AA, whereas RNAi caused opposite changes. 4. Incorporation into engineered yeast enabled de novo DHAA production, validating pathway utility for semi-synthetic artemisinin.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful enzyme for metabolic engineering to convert high-yield AA into DHAA and improve semi-synthetic artemisinin production; can be stacked with upstream artemisinin-pathway genes in yeast or plant chassis.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species: Artemisia annua; Saccharomyces cerevisiae; Nicotiana benthamiana
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Artemisia annua; Saccharomyces cerevisiae; Nicotiana benthamiana
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Engineered oxidoreductase mutant; DBR2-like double-bond reductase/dehydrogenase
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Downstream/terminal branch from artemisinic acid to dihydroartemisinic acid
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Engineered P26L variant with enhanced catalytic conversion of artemisinic acid to dihydroartemisinic acid and strongly reduced reverse oxidation activity.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms key intermediate DHAA required for artemisinin formation
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Engineered bottleneck-improving step for DHAA production
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Increases DHAA production more strongly than wild-type AaDHAADH; 2.82-fold higher activity toward AA; enabled 3.96-3.97 g/L DHAA in 5 L engineered yeast fermentation and boosted DHAA in A. annua cells.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Antimalarial drug; also reported anti-tumor, immunomodulatory, anti-inflammatory and antiviral activities
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Glandular hairs of above-ground parts of Artemisia annua, highest in leaves
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Site-directed mutagenesis; enzyme assays; kinetic analysis; molecular docking; MM-GBSA free-energy analysis; overexpression in A. annua cells; pathway engineering in S. cerevisiae; transient pathway reconstruction in Nicotiana benthamiana
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: UV treatment in N. benthamiana enabled downstream artemisinin detection from produced DHAA; optimal in vitro activity at pH 7.5 and 30 °C
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: CaMV35S promoter in A. annua cells and N. benthamiana; THI2 promoter used for additional copy expression in S. cerevisiae
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Reverse conversion of DHAA to AA was largely suppressed (0.03 times wild type), which is beneficial for DHAA accumulation; no additional fitness penalty explicitly established
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: P26 is a key catalytic residue. P26L lowered Km for AA to 170.29 μM and increased kcat/Km to 5.2850×10^-5 min^-1·μM^-1.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. P26 was identified as a key residue affecting substrate binding and catalysis. 2. The P26L mutation generated the best-performing variant for AA-to-DHAA conversion. 3. This variant displayed 2.82-fold higher activity toward AA and strongly diminished reverse activity. 4. Its deployment in yeast and tobacco chassis substantially improved DHAA synthesis and enabled artemisinin detection after UV treatment in tobacco.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Preferred engineered allele for industrial semi-synthetic artemisinin production because it channels AA to DHAA more efficiently and suppresses reverse flux; suitable for gene stacking in microbial or plant production systems.
